chapters.title: We must knock down the stigma around mental health
chapters.text:
These days, we're encouraged to talk more openly about mental health, which can only be a positive development. In the '40s and beyond, mental health was whispered about behind closed doors. Such was the case for Don and Mimi Galvin and their large brood of twelve children. Six out of the ten boys were diagnosed with schizophrenia during a time when such conditions were largely misunderstood.

The Galvin family's plight quickly became a mission to understand more about schizophrenia and mental health as a whole. Despite their heartbreak and loss, with one son, Brian, killing his girlfriend and himself during a psychotic episode, their story prompted a serious conversation that needed to be had. To understand mental health means to ask tough questions.

Schizophrenia is a psychiatric disorder summarized by continuous or relapsing episodes of psychosis. Several repeated psychotic breaks describe it, sometimes throughout the individual's entire life. It can also manifest as a constant stream of psychosis, causing its victim to live a life of continual detachment from reality, often in a constant state of delusion. The condition causes a breakdown in the relation between thought, emotion, and behavior, leading to faulty perceptions and inappropriate actions and feelings. People with the condition tend to exist in their own “worlds” and are always caught up in obscure behavioral patterns.

Despite the fact that a stigma around mental health still exists, a vast 100,000 people are diagnosed with the condition every year in the U.S. alone.

There is light at the end of the tunnel, however. Once diagnosed, an individual can receive treatment and lead an everyday life. The main issue is a refusal to seek help, and that many friends or family members create excuses for their loved ones, who postpone treatment. In many cases, this is what happened with the Galvins.

While it's probably an impossible task to knock down hundreds of years of stigma overnight, Hidden Valley Road starts a meaningful conversation. By telling the story of a typical family, pulled apart by the sheer lack of knowledge of what was going on, perhaps others will see similarities in themselves or loved ones and seek help as a result.

chapters.title: Schizophrenia is a complex condition with many potential causes
chapters.text:
The Galvins had twelve children in total, but 20 years after the birth of their first child, when their final baby came into the world, there were signs that many of the boys were struggling with something they didn't quite understand. Of course, at the time, there was a considerable lack of understanding about mental health conditions as a whole, let alone something as complex as schizophrenia.

The causes of schizophrenia are wide-ranging, yet none are conclusive enough to pinpoint an exact risk factor. Of course, there are many “educated guesses” that will at least narrow it down to some genuine and likely causes.

Several of the Galvin boys were diagnosed with schizophrenia. This led to the theory that the condition can be passed down through DNA.

The most likely cause is associated with the brain and its shape, size, or chemistry. Abnormal functioning of neurotransmitters, mainly dopamine, can cause schizophrenia. There is a theory that the onset of schizophrenia can be attributed to a malfunction in the dopamine receptors. Overall, however, upset in the brain's chemical balance is one of the likely causes of the condition.

Since the condition's causes aren't concrete, sometimes it can pass from an individual to their offspring. Heredity as a cause of schizophrenia only covers the fact that it exists and didn’t appear as a stroke of luck. As many of the Galvin's boys began to show examples of troublesome behavior and symptoms, the idea of schizophrenia being hereditary began to take hold.

Pregnancy complications can also be a factor to consider. But these causes are not set in stone, and many individuals diagnosed with schizophrenia often have a link to at least one of them. That means they are at least a good enough “guess” and can be used to formulate a safe and effective treatment.

Regardless of the cause of schizophrenia, there needs to be more focus on encouraging people to reach out for help, rather than hiding away or pretending nothing is happening.

chapters.title: The symptoms of schizophrenia can often be confused for other conditions on the mental health spectrum
chapters.text:
These days, we have a much greater insight into mental health conditions and their symptoms. Yet, when the Galvins were struggling with six sons, all diagnosed with schizophrenia or, at least, showing signs of psychotic behavior, many were at a loss about what to do. With constant fights, many turning violent, and episodes of extreme delusion and psychosis, it took a long time for their schizophrenia to be diagnosed.

Many people live with schizophrenia without ever being diagnosed. Reaching out for help is vital for treatment to begin.

Even now, the condition is easy to misinterpret or confuse with other similar conditions. However, it’s essential to identify it amongst the multitude of disorders that share similar signs. But the only way to do that is to ascertain the symptoms that are particular to the condition itself.

Many mental disorders have similar symptoms, but there are unique ones for schizophrenia.
• Delusion— a distinct belief or impression maintained despite being contradicted by reality or rational argument. It is a detachment from reality.
• Hallucinations — chief symptoms of most mental disorders but very present in schizophrenia.
• Agitation — a steady and unwavering state of unrest.
• Inability to express emotions — apathy or the absence of emotion often confused with sociopathy, also a symptom of schizophrenia.
• Disordered thinking — the lack of cognizance in thought and understanding.
• Inappropriate reactions — an increase in rash and irrational reactions and behaviors.
• Phobias — more present as the hallucinations and delusions continue to build.
• Lack of pleasure or interest in activities — a general disinterest in things that many people find pleasurable.

To be diagnosed with schizophrenia, an individual must exhibit these symptoms, either isolated or as a combination of two or more. Because schizophrenia's range of symptoms can overlap with other conditions, these are the main ones to look out for.

chapters.title: Schizophrenia affects the individual and those around them
chapters.text:
Of course, if someone has a mental health condition of any type, life is tough. With schizophrenia, an individual often has a steady state of disjointed reality. While treatment can help them to live a relatively normal life, the worry of an episode occurring is always there in the background. However, it's easy to forget that those close to the individual also have a tough time.

Mimi Galvin tried her best to manage all her children at home, despite six being diagnosed with schizophrenia. With regular violent outbursts and the uncertainty of irregular behavior becoming somewhat of a norm in the household, two of the girls struggled. Both Mary and Margaret were primarily affected by what was happening to their brothers, and as they grew up and left home, they struggled to deal with their ordeals.

The focus is on the diagnosed person, but those in their close circle can often struggle with mental health difficulties of their own due to their experiences.

While Joe and Matt underwent treatment and responded very well, the other sons either didn't engage with treatment or didn't respond well. This caused a regular occurrence of unpredictable behavior and violence within the household.

Our relationships can destroy us, but they can change us, too, and restore us, and without us ever seeing it happen, they define us. ~ Robert Kolker

It's vital to remember that a person with schizophrenia has a reality far removed from anything and anyone around them, often acting without consideration for their welfare or that of others. And that can be extremely hurtful for family members who are simply trying to help.

Did you know? Approximately 1.1% of the world's population is affected by schizophrenia.

chapters.title: Schizophrenia is steeped in confusion and misunderstanding
chapters.text:
It's vital to remember that a person struggling with schizophrenia has no control over their thoughts or actions during an episode. It can be hard to understand and come to grips with if you've never spent a significant amount of time with someone who has this diagnosis. As such, there are many misconceptions surrounding schizophrenia and how it works.

Of course, the main reason is that it bears a striking resemblance to quite a few other mental illnesses and, as such, can be wrongfully diagnosed if there isn't a trained or watchful eye on the job.

The first misconception is that a person with schizophrenia has multiple personalities. This is not true. This misconception results from the erratic behavior or attitudes a person with the condition can often show. Multiple personalities are also a prominent symptom for MPD (Multiple Personalities Disorder), but schizophrenia retains one personality broken into tiny pieces.

The second myth or misconception is that people with schizophrenia all experience a similar set of symptoms. This is also not true. Schizophrenia is such that individuals experience a varying degree of symptoms. No two persons have the same behavioral pattern. This is evident in the Galvin sons. All were diagnosed with schizophrenia, but no two experienced exactly the same symptoms.

And what about the thousands of people who couldn’t afford what her sons had—who languish because of a lack of resources, or a stigma from a society that would prefer to pretend that people like them do not exist? ~ Robert Kolker

Lastly, another misconception is that schizophrenia can be caused by how a child was treated growing up, their environment, and their interactions. This is mainly false; the conditions for schizophrenia are so random that it is literally a guess sometimes. No single event can cause it. If that were the case, why would only a handful of the Galvin children experience the condition and not all?

It's essential to understand schizophrenia, and other mental health conditions, to help knock down the stigma. We often fear what we don't know.

chapters.title: The good news is that schizophrenia can be treated and managed
chapters.text:
Two of the Galvin boys responded well to treatment and lived a relatively free life once diagnosed. However, that wasn't the case for all of them. When a person doesn't reach out for help or doesn't want to receive treatment, the condition will always win out. The other issue is that, at the time of the boys' diagnoses, we didn't have the range of treatments that we have today.

The range of treatments for schizophrenia has increased over the years. With more research and understanding, the chances of an individual with the condition living a relatively unaffected life increase.

The cornerstone for treating schizophrenia is medication; it is without a doubt the first point of call for an assured and temporal solution. Why? Because much of the illness' zone of effect is the brain, primarily the brain's chemistry. The medications chosen are aptly named “antipsychotics.”

The targets of these antipsychotics are the brain's neurotransmitters responsible for dopamine reception. This helps create a balance and reduces the tendency to experience any psychotic episodes as a result. While medications work, the side effects often prevent people from wanting to try them, often resulting in individuals opting for injections instead.

Another safe and surefire way to treat schizophrenia is psychosocial intervention, e.g., therapy. This therapy can be in the following forms:
• Psychotherapy, which may help to normalize thought patterns and help the individual handle stress.
• Social skills training focuses on improving communication and social interactions.
• Family therapy provides support to families dealing with schizophrenia.

When treatment fails, or the individual becomes a danger to themselves, their physician can opt for hospitalization. As such, the risk to themselves and others reduces.

A person cannot seek help for any condition, schizophrenia or otherwise, unless they want to do so themselves. Of course, if that person becomes dangerous, a doctor can intervene with hospitalization, but the decision to reach out for help should be their own.

Mimi Galvin did her best to look after her sons at home, but ultimately, the decision for treatment had to be theirs.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Schizophrenia is more widespread in the United States than you might think. Of course, this extends across the world too. Despite its prevalence, there is still a considerable amount of confusion and misunderstanding around the condition. Unfortunately, many people live with it until they eventually die, suffering needlessly throughout their whole lives.

Schizophrenia is often ignored, misinterpreted, and even wrongfully diagnosed, but it remains a genuine and present problem. The symptoms are easy to miss or create excuses for, but they are real and indicate a vast disconnect between the individual and the reality we all share. The Galvin family attracted attention because of the potential heredity link. Having shared their story, we now know far more than we knew before, which can only benefit those diagnosed in the future.

The bottom line is that the struggles of living with the condition are not talked about enough, and worse is the mental fortitude it takes to be a caregiver to a person with schizophrenia. Mimi Galvin sacrificed her life to look after her affected sons, while the rest of the family constantly struggled to deal with outbursts and the potential for an episode to occur.

With such a large number of people diagnosed with schizophrenia every year, it's vital that we knock down the stigma, learn more about the condition, and give help and support to the individual and the immediate family. We might never wholly understand mental health conditions in their entirety, but we can do our best to help make life easier for those affected.

Try this

• Remember to check in with your loved ones regularly. Ask if they're okay, talk to them, and don't ignore the warning signs.

• Create an open communication environment in your household. Mental health is not something we should avoid talking about; we all have it, after all!

• Educate yourself on different mental health conditions and learn to recognize the signs. We should all be more knowledgeable to help others.

Read it till the end?
